name: Dallas County, Texas
tagline: Texas' second-most-populous county (~2.6M residents) runs centralized technology through its IT Services department — buying largely off pre-competed cooperative vehicles, including DIR, and rebuilding its security posture after a 2023 ransomware hit.
overview: Dallas County is the second-most-populous county in Texas, serving roughly 2.6 million residents across Dallas and surrounding communities. Like most large Texas counties it is governed by a five-member Commissioners Court — a County Judge elected at large plus four district commissioners — which sets the budget and approves major technology purchases. Central technology is delivered by the county's IT Services department, which supports county-wide infrastructure, the justice and public-safety systems, elections technology, health and human services, and resident-facing digital services. The county's technology priorities were reshaped by an October 2023 ransomware incident (attributed to the 'Play' group) that exposed data on roughly 200,000 individuals; the county has since increased IT funding and, in December 2024, named a permanent CIO after more than a year without one. For a Dell SLG seller, Dallas County is a large, centralized county buyer with active cybersecurity, modernization, and resilience drivers — and one that purchases heavily through cooperative contracts rather than one-off RFPs.

budget.intro: Dallas County adopts an annual all-funds operating and capital budget (recent adopted budgets have run in the ~$1.7B–$1.8B range; confirm the exact FY2026 all-funds total in the county's published budget). Technology is a distinct and growing line: the IT Services department budget was reported to have risen from roughly $58M to over $70M as the county responded to its security incident. The FY2026 adopted property tax rate is $0.215500 per $100 of assessed valuation.

stats.label: IT Services budget
stats.note: Reported recent figure, up from ~$58M; confirm current-year line

stats.label: County all-funds budget
stats.note: Recent adopted-budget range; verify exact FY2026 total

stats.label: FY2026 tax rate
stats.note: Per $100 assessed valuation (M&O + I&S), Tax Year 2025

notes: IT spending rose materially following the October 2023 ransomware incident — security and resilience are funded priorities, not aspirations.
Exact current-year IT and all-funds totals should be confirmed against the county's published FY2026 budget documents before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Post-incident cybersecurity rebuild
initiatives.summary: After the October 2023 'Play' ransomware incident (data on ~200K individuals exposed), the county increased IT funding and refocused on endpoint monitoring, access controls, and resilience. Security modernization is the clearest active driver — lead with it.

initiatives.name: IT leadership reset and governance
initiatives.summary: A permanent CIO (Justine Tran) was named in December 2024 after a long vacancy. Expect formalized IT governance, ITIL-style service management, and a re-baselining of infrastructure — an opening for refresh and standardization conversations.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure and data-center modernization
initiatives.summary: A large county supporting justice, elections, health, and resident services runs aging infrastructure that the new IT leadership is positioned to refresh. Server, storage, and hybrid-cloud modernization are natural ISG entry points.

initiatives.name: Resident-facing digital services
initiatives.summary: The county continues to expand online services (records, payments, justice and health portals). Backend compute, storage, and identity/security underpin these — connect endpoint and infrastructure plays to the citizen-services roadmap.

procurement.intro: Dallas County buys through its Purchasing Department, which processes thousands of purchase orders and hundreds of formal solicitations each year and posts opportunities via the BidNet platform (adopted December 2024). For technology, the county — like other Texas local governments — can buy voluntarily off DIR Cooperative Contracts and other cooperative vehicles, avoiding a full RFP. Confirm Dell's DIR and co-op coverage and register as a supplier before pursuing direct awards.

vehicles.name: Dallas County Purchasing (BidNet)
vehicles.detail: Central purchasing for the county; solicitations are posted through BidNet (live since Dec 2024). Vendors must register to receive notices and bid.

vehicles.name: DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: Texas local governments may buy IT products and services voluntarily off DIR's 650+ pre-competed co-op contracts at negotiated discounts — a fast path that avoids a county RFP.

vehicles.name: Other cooperative purchasing
vehicles.detail: National/local co-ops (e.g., interlocal and purchasing cooperatives) are also available to the county for technology and services — confirm which vehicle is preferred for a given category.

powerStructure.name: Clay Jenkins
powerStructure.title: Dallas County Judge
powerStructure.note: Presiding officer of the Commissioners Court; sets tone and presides over budget and major-purchase approvals — the top political relationship.

powerStructure.name: Commissioners Court
powerStructure.title: John Wiley Price, Theresa Daniel, Elba Garcia, Andrew Sommerman
powerStructure.note: Four district commissioners who, with the Judge, approve the budget and large technology contracts.

powerStructure.name: Justine Tran
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (IT Services)
powerStructure.note: Named CIO Dec 2024 after a long vacancy; background includes City of Dallas tech strategy and ITIL governance. The primary technology relationship.

powerStructure.name: IT Services security leadership
powerStructure.title: Information security / CISO function
powerStructure.note: Owns the post-incident security program (monitoring, access, resilience). Confirm the current named CISO/security lead before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Dallas County Purchasing Department
powerStructure.title: Central procurement / solicitations
powerStructure.note: Owns BidNet solicitations, vendor registration, and co-op usage — the procedural path onto any deal.

news.title: Dallas County names Justine Tran permanent CIO after 1+ year vacancy
news.impact: Stable IT leadership returns; expect governance, modernization, and security-program momentum — re-engage the technology buying center.

news.title: County moves solicitations to the BidNet platform
news.impact: All goods/services/construction solicitations now post through BidNet; vendors must register there to bid.

news.title: Ransomware incident ('Play') exposes data on ~200K individuals
news.impact: Triggered increased IT/security funding and a multi-year resilience focus — the anchor for cybersecurity and infrastructure plays.

dellActions: Lead with cybersecurity and resilience — the 2023 ransomware incident and subsequent funding increase make security the county's clearest active driver.
Build the relationship with CIO Justine Tran and the IT Services team; tie infrastructure refresh and standardization to the post-vacancy governance reset.
Confirm Dell's DIR Cooperative Contract and other co-op coverage so the county can buy without a full RFP, and register as a supplier in BidNet.
Connect ISG (compute, storage, hybrid cloud) and endpoint/security offers to justice, elections, health, and resident-services workloads.
Verify exact current-year IT and all-funds budget figures against the published FY2026 documents before sizing opportunities.
